followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the prior 'promising' verdict but lower the overall score slightly because the other reviews sharpened concrete systematics: phonon/background thermal conductance, charge-puddle limits on n -> 0, hydrodynamic-regime windows, and boundary-condition dependence in eta/s. I still reject treating the incomplete bundle as proof that the underlying paper is technically unsound.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini's 'fatal' technical objection is too strong if read as a flaw in the paper itself; it is a fatal limitation of this review bundle, not demonstrated evidence that the Nature Physics paper is wrong.
Gemini's novelty assessment is too generous from the abstract alone, because the bundle itself cites prior WF-law breakdown and quantum-critical conductivity experiments in graphene.

Claude's exact displayed hydrodynamic formula should not be accepted without checking the paper and cited theory; the safe objection is that the relation is not a simple model-free inverse relation, not that a particular alternative formula has been established from the bundle.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Claude's emphasis on phonon or substrate heat conductance, electron-phonon cooling, and noise-thermometry calibration is a real major concern for the 200x Lorentz-number claim.

Claude's point that the simple 'inverse relation' between electrical and thermal conductivity is likely shorthand for a more constrained hydrodynamic formula is valid and should be checked against Eq. (3).
Claude's charge-puddle/n* concern is directly grounded in the Extended Data Fig. 4 caption mentioning intrinsic charge inhomogeneity and Supplementary Table I.
Claude's boundary-condition concern for absolute viscosity extraction is valid given the slip/no-slip sensitivity in graphene hydrodynamics.
Gemini is right that the supplied bundle lacks the full manuscript and that this sharply limits review confidence.

main_claims.claim: Near the Dirac point, graphene realizes a quantum-critical Dirac-fluid regime whose charge and heat flow can be modeled using relativistic hydrodynamic transport coefficients and thermodynamic densities.
main_claims.notes: The theoretical framing is plausible and well situated in the cited literature, but the bundle does not show the paper's regime checks, scattering hierarchy, or derivation of the fitted transport formulae.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract, first two sentences; Fig. 1 caption; references to Son 2007, Hartnoll et al. 2007, Fritz et al. 2008, Lucas et al. 2016

main_claims.claim: Electrical and thermal conductivities are inversely related near the Dirac point, as expected from relativistic hydrodynamics.
main_claims.notes: Other reviewers correctly emphasize that a simple inverse relation is likely shorthand for a more structured hydrodynamic relation involving the intrinsic conductivity, entropy/enthalpy, density, temperature, and momentum relaxation. The bundle lacks Eq. (3), residuals, and domain-of-validity checks.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 2 caption; Fig. 3 caption; Extended Data Fig. 3-5 captions

main_claims.claim: The characteristic quantum-critical conductivity converges to a quantized universal value.
main_claims.notes: This is the central claim. The visible text does not state the numerical value, units, uncertainty, theoretical target, fitting/extrapolation method, or robustness to residual density inhomogeneity. Prior work by Gallagher et al. 2019 makes the novelty/quantization comparison especially important.

main_claims.claim: The Lorentz number exceeds the semiclassical Wiedemann-Franz value by more than 200 times near the Dirac point at low temperatures.
main_claims.notes: The claim is explicit and supported in outline by density- and temperature-dependent extended-data captions. The magnitude is large enough that noise thermometry calibration, electron-phonon cooling, hBN/substrate phonon heat paths, contacts, and geometric gradients need direct audit.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 2 caption; Extended Data Fig. 2-5 captions

main_claims.claim: At high temperatures, the effective dynamic viscosity-to-entropy-density ratio approaches the minimally viscous quantum-fluid value within a factor of four.
main_claims.notes: Multiple-device consistency in Extended Data Fig. 6 is encouraging, but the operational definition of eta_th, entropy-density input, boundary condition, and uncertainty budget are absent from the bundle.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 4 caption; Extended Data Fig. 6 caption

missing_assumptions.assumption: Residual charge inhomogeneity is small enough that the analyzed near-Dirac-point regime is genuinely quantum critical rather than puddle dominated.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Extended Data Fig. 4 caption and Supplementary Table I reference
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A finite disorder density scale can mimic or mask saturation of conductivity and can change the inferred Lorentz ratio near charge neutrality.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The thermal signal used for kappa_e separates electronic heat transport from substrate/hBN phonons, electron-phonon cooling, and contact heat leakage.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Extended Data Fig. 2 caption; Fig. 2 caption; Supplementary Sections 1-10 listing
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Any uncorrected parallel heat path directly biases the Lorentz number and the claimed magnitude of Wiedemann-Franz violation.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Electron-electron scattering dominates over impurity, boundary, ballistic, and phonon processes in the windows used for hydrodynamic fits.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Fig. 1 caption; Extended Data Fig. 1 caption; Abstract
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The relativistic-hydrodynamic formulae and viscosity interpretation require a specific hierarchy of scattering lengths/times.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The theoretical 'quantized value' is specified in the same degeneracy, unit, interaction, and finite-temperature convention as the experimentally extracted characteristic conductivity.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without a precise target and convention, convergence to a plateau is weaker evidence than convergence to a universal quantum-critical conductivity.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The entropy and enthalpy densities used in the analysis correspond to the same finite-temperature, interacting, weakly disordered graphene system measured experimentally.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The extracted viscosity-to-entropy ratio and hydrodynamic conductivity relations can shift if clean/noninteracting thermodynamic inputs are used outside their validity range.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Boundary conditions and device geometry are independently constrained in the viscosity extraction.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Fig. 4 caption; Extended Data Fig. 1 and Extended Data Fig. 6 captions
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Slip/no-slip assumptions and geometry factors can change the absolute viscosity by order-one factors, which matters for the claimed proximity to a minimal-viscosity bound.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Fig. 3 and the corresponding text show convergence to a specific predicted quantum-critical conductivity value, with uncertainty, degeneracy/unit conventions, and comparison to Gallagher et al. 2019?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The bundle does not include Fig. 3 data, the numerical value, or the analysis text.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How do the methods separate electronic thermal conductivity from hBN/substrate phonons, electron-phonon cooling, contact heat leakage, and spatial temperature gradients in the noise-thermometry analysis?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The required Eq. (6), calibration procedure, and supplementary discussion are referenced but absent.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the density and temperature windows used for the universal-conductivity and Lorentz-number fits demonstrably inside the hydrodynamic regime rather than Knudsen/ballistic crossover?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Only the Extended Data Fig. 1 caption is visible; the scattering lengths, device widths, and fit windows are not provided.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the residual density inhomogeneity n* small compared with the fitted n0 and analyzed near-neutrality densities for each device?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The visible text mentions intrinsic charge inhomogeneity and Supplementary Table I but does not provide the table or values.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How is eta_th/s_th normalized relative to the KSS/minimal-viscosity convention, and how sensitive is it to slip length, geometry, entropy-density model, and disorder corrections?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The bundle lacks definitions of eta_th and s_th and the detailed viscosity extraction.

novelty_assessment: The conceptual framework is not new: the cited literature already contains quantum-critical Dirac-fluid theory, Wiedemann-Franz-law breakdown in graphene, prior quantum-critical conductivity measurements, thermal diffusivity work, and hydrodynamic/viscous-flow evidence. The plausible novelty is experimental: combining electrical and thermal transport in ultraclean devices to extract the intrinsic conductivity more cleanly, reporting a much larger Lorentz-number enhancement, and linking transport to eta/s in the same device class. That is a meaningful advance if the methods hold up, but it is not a wholly new phenomenon and the broad 'universality' language needs quantitative support beyond the visible abstract/captions.
score_rationale: The paper's claims are significant and plausibly valuable, especially if the combined electrical/thermal extraction really pins down the intrinsic quantum-critical conductivity. However, the review evidence remains thin: the core equations, figures, methods, supplementary tables, and source-data analysis are absent. The other reviewers add legitimate systematic concerns, but do not demonstrate a fatal flaw. I therefore score it as a promising but materially caveated experimental advance rather than a strong select or weak-evidence rejection.
summary: The supplied bundle is still a publisher landing-page/preview scrape for an experimental Nature Physics paper on charge and heat transport in ultraclean graphene. The headline claims are potentially important: extraction of a universal quantum-critical conductivity, a very large Wiedemann-Franz-law violation, and a viscosity-to-entropy ratio near the minimal-viscosity scale. After reading the other reviews, I keep the main caution from cycle 1: the visible text is insufficient to audit the quantitative reductions. I add more weight to systematic concerns around phonon/background thermal conductance, charge puddles, hydrodynamic-regime windows, and boundary-condition dependence in viscosity extraction. I do not accept the view that the underlying paper should be scored as technically failed solely because the review bundle is incomplete; that is a limitation of the provided evidence, not a demonstrated flaw in the paper.

technical_objections.evidence: The supplied text moves from abstract to access options, figure captions, extended-data captions, references, and metadata. It references equations and supplementary analyses that are not included.
technical_objections.objection: The review bundle omits the full main text, methods, equations, supplementary sections, and source-data details needed to audit the central quantitative claims.
technical_objections.paper_location: Access notice after abstract; Supplementary Information listing; references to Eq. (3), Eq. (6), Supplementary Sections 1-10

technical_objections.evidence: Real graphene near charge neutrality has finite density inhomogeneity. If the plateau is set by n* or disorder-limited transport rather than the quantum-critical intrinsic conductivity, the universal interpretation weakens substantially.
technical_objections.objection: The quantized/universal conductivity claim is not auditable without the numerical target, uncertainty, and treatment of residual disorder and charge puddles.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 3 caption; Extended Data Fig. 4-5 captions mentioning intrinsic charge inhomogeneity, n0, and Timp

technical_objections.evidence: The reported factor greater than 200 relies on electronic temperature/noise thermometry and inferred heat transport. Parallel phonon heat conductance, electron-phonon cooling, contact thermal resistance, Joule-power calibration, and spatial temperature profiles could materially alter the Lorentz number if not controlled.
technical_objections.objection: The large Wiedemann-Franz violation depends on a model-sensitive thermal-conductivity extraction whose systematics are not visible here.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 2 caption; Extended Data Fig. 2 caption on electronic temperature and Eq. (6); Extended Data Fig. 3-5 captions

technical_objections.evidence: The visible caption shows a crossover from Knudsen to Poiseuille behavior, but does not establish that all fitted quantum-critical conductivity and heat-flow data lie in the collision-dominated hydrodynamic regime rather than ballistic/crossover regimes.
technical_objections.objection: The hydrodynamic interpretation requires a demonstrated scattering-length hierarchy across the same density and temperature windows used for the universality claims.
technical_objections.paper_location: Fig. 1 caption; Extended Data Fig. 1 caption on Knudsen-to-Poiseuille crossover; Abstract

technical_objections.evidence: The bundle does not define eta_th or s_th, show the extraction method, state boundary/slip assumptions, or propagate geometric and thermodynamic uncertainties. Factors of order two to four would be decisive for a 'within a factor of four' claim.
technical_objections.objection: The viscosity-to-entropy comparison to a minimal-viscosity scale may be strongly model-dependent.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 4 caption; Extended Data Fig. 6 caption

technical_objections.evidence: The reference list includes Crossno et al. 2016 on Dirac-fluid/WF-law breakdown and Gallagher et al. 2019 on quantum-critical conductivity. The new contribution appears to be a cleaner combined electrical/thermal extraction, not the first observation of the general phenomena.
technical_objections.objection: The novelty statement risks being overbroad unless the paper sharply distinguishes its result from earlier graphene Dirac-fluid and quantum-critical-conductivity experiments.

technical_objections.evidence: Several device labels appear, but the bundle does not give selection criteria, full device yield, mobility/inhomogeneity table, or whether all devices support the same universal value within uncertainty.
technical_objections.objection: The evidence for device-independent universality is incompletely specified in the visible text.
technical_objections.paper_location: Title; Abstract; Extended Data Fig. 6 caption listing additional devices D3S5, D5S5, D1S6, D4S6; Extended Data Fig. 4-5 captions
